title: Particles in the atom and atomic radius
summary: Free Cambridge 9701 AS Chemistry — protons, neutrons, electrons, atomic number, mass number, ions, and trends in atomic radius with a live Build an Atom PhET simulation.
learningObjectives: Describe the relative charges and masses of protons, neutrons, and electrons.
Define atomic number, mass number, and relative atomic mass.
Explain how ions form and deduce the number of subatomic particles in atoms and ions.
Describe the trend in atomic radius across a period and down a group.

simpleExplanation.title: Building blocks of matter
simpleExplanation.summary: Every atom has a dense positive nucleus (protons + neutrons) surrounded by electrons in shells. The PhET sim lets you assemble atoms and see how particle counts determine identity, mass, and charge.
steps: Add protons to set the element — atomic number Z equals the proton count.
Add neutrons to change mass number A without changing which element it is.
Balance electrons with protons for a neutral atom; gain or lose electrons to form ions.
Compare atoms across Period 3 — nuclear charge pulls electrons closer, so radius decreases left to right.

flashcards.front: Relative mass and charge of a proton?
flashcards.back: Mass ≈ 1; charge +1 (same magnitude as electron, opposite sign).
Number of protons in the nucleus — defines the element.
Total number of protons + neutrons in the nucleus.

flashcards.front: How many electrons in the Fe³⁺ ion (Z = 26)?
Trend in atomic radius across Period 3?
flashcards.back: Decreases — increasing nuclear charge attracts electrons more strongly.

flashcards.front: Trend in atomic radius down Group 1?
flashcards.back: Increases — additional electron shells outweigh increased nuclear charge.

sections.content: Atomic structure is the foundation of **9701 Chemistry**. Examiners expect precise definitions, correct subatomic counts in ions, and clear explanations of **periodic trends**. Use the **PhET Build an Atom** simulation below to connect particle counts to symbols and charges.
Mass number A = protons + neutrons. Atomic number Z = protons. In a neutral atom: electrons = protons. Ion charge = protons − electrons.

items: Proton number defines the element — changing it changes the element
Isotopes have the same Z but different neutron numbers (see topic 1.2)
Most mass is in the nucleus; most volume is empty space with electrons
Atomic radius is a measure of the size of the outer electron shell

sections.question: An ion contains 53 protons, 74 neutrons, and 54 electrons. Identify the ion and its mass number.
sections.solution:
Z = 53 → iodine (I).

Mass number A = 53 + 74 = 127.

Charge = 53 − 54 = −1 → ion is **¹²⁷₅₃I⁻**.

sections.content: In subatomic counting questions, write a mini table: protons, neutrons, electrons, charge. Examiners often trap candidates who forget that **mass number applies to the nucleus only**, not the whole atom including electrons.

steps.caption: Atoms contain protons and neutrons in the nucleus, electrons in shells.
steps.embedHint: Add particles one at a time — read atomic number and mass number.

steps.caption: Atomic number Z = proton number; mass number A = protons + neutrons.
steps.embedHint: Build a neutral atom — electrons should equal protons.

steps.caption: Ions form when electrons are gained or lost — charge = protons − electrons.
steps.embedHint: Remove or add electrons — watch the charge indicator change.

steps.caption: Atomic radius decreases across a period (increasing nuclear charge).
steps.embedHint: Compare neutral atoms of neighbouring elements in the same period.
